content.text: Unit testing is the foundation of a reliable software development process.

In agentic AI systems — where an LLM drives multi-step reasoning, dispatches tool calls, and accumulates state across a conversation — untested code is especially dangerous because failures can propagate through many layers before surfacing.
This topic teaches you to write fast, deterministic unit tests for every deterministic piece of your agent stack, so that the only part you leave to probabilistic evaluation is the model itself.
exercises a single function, class, or module in complete isolation from its dependencies.
For ordinary software this means replacing database connections and HTTP clients with mocks.
For agentic AI it means something additional: replacing the
with a mock that returns scripted, deterministic responses, so that the logic
the model — input validation, tool dispatch, state management, output parsing — can be tested cheaply, quickly, and without variability.
The key insight is that an agent consists of two distinct categories of code. The first is
: prompt builders, tool routers, retry logic, output parsers, state machines. Every line of this code can and should be covered by ordinary unit tests. The second is
invocation. This part belongs to evals, not unit tests. Unit testing draws a clean boundary between the two categories and tests only the first.
The classical definition comes from Michael Feathers in
: a unit test runs fast, does not touch the network or file system, does not require special environment setup, and is fully deterministic. All four properties hold for well-written agent unit tests.

Agentic systems fail in subtle ways. A prompt-construction bug might silently truncate the tool list for long contexts. An output parser might accept malformed JSON under some regex paths but not others. A retry loop might not actually re-send the tool result in the correct message format. None of these failures require a broken model — they are pure logic bugs in Python or TypeScript code that a unit test catches immediately.

Without unit tests, developers must run the entire agent against a live LLM to verify a change, which costs money (every token counts), takes seconds per call, and produces non-deterministic output. A flaky test caused by model temperature cannot tell you whether your code change introduced a bug. A suite of unit tests gives you a deterministic signal in milliseconds.
Unit tests also act as executable documentation. A test named

communicates the contract — the loop is bounded — more precisely than any comment. When a new contributor changes the agent loop, the test enforces the contract without requiring a code review to catch the missing guard.
Unit tests backed by mocks run in milliseconds and incur zero API cost, enabling you to run hundreds of tests on every pull request without a budget concern.
Mocked LLM responses are fixed, so a test that passed yesterday passes today regardless of model drift, API outages, or temperature variance.
When a unit test fails the bug is in one function. Compare this to an end-to-end test failure that could originate anywhere in the stack.
A comprehensive unit test suite lets you rename a parameter, restructure a class, or swap an HTTP library with confidence that behaviour is preserved.
It is easy to write a test for the
rejection path or the empty-query guard. Exercising those branches manually through a real agent conversation is impractical.
Tests encode the expected interface of every function: what it accepts, what it returns, what it raises, and what side effects it produces.
Unit tests do not catch every class of bug. Their limitations matter especially in agentic systems.
Whether the model chooses the right tool, produces coherent reasoning, or follows the system prompt is completely invisible to a unit test. You need evals and human review for this.
object with a structure that no longer matches the real SDK after an API version bump, tests stay green while production breaks. Mocks require maintenance.
returns the right structure, but it cannot verify that the agent correctly serialises the tool result back into the messages array. Integration tests cover wiring.

Tests that assert on internal implementation details (specific call counts, internal field names) become brittle and require updates with every internal refactor, even when observable behaviour is unchanged.
A 100% unit-test pass rate on agent orchestration code gives no signal about whether the system actually solves the user's task correctly. Unit tests are necessary but not sufficient.

Think of your agentic system as a factory assembly line. The LLM is one specific machine on the line — a sophisticated black box that takes raw material (prompts) and produces output (text or tool calls). Unit testing is
about testing the machine itself. It is about testing every
conveyor belt, router, sensor, and quality-control gate

that surrounds that machine. You test: does the input arrive formatted correctly? Does the router send the right package to the right downstream station? Does the error gate actually stop the line when a part fails validation? The machine has its own evaluation process at the quality lab. Your assembly-line code has unit tests.

A practical heuristic: if a line of code makes a decision that does not require calling the LLM API, it belongs under a unit test. If a line of code passes data to or receives data from the LLM, it belongs in a mock boundary — test the code on
Every unit test follows the same three-phase structure.
: set up the system under test, inject mocks, prepare input data.
: call the function under test exactly once.
: verify the output, the state changes, and the side effects (calls made to mocks).
The core technique that makes agent code testable is
: instead of constructing the Anthropic client inside the agent class (
), accept it as a constructor parameter. The production caller passes the real client; the test passes a
for cases where injection is not possible.
Line coverage measures what fraction of source lines are executed by the test suite. It is useful as a minimum bar but not as a quality signal.

attrs.latex: \text{Line Coverage} = \frac{|\text{lines executed by tests}|}{|\text{total executable lines}|} \times 100\%

content.text: Branch coverage is a stronger metric for agent code because it counts each conditional branch separately:
has two branches. 100% line coverage is achievable by hitting the
branch — perhaps the error handler — goes untested. Branch coverage of
is a reasonable target for agent orchestration code; aim for
on utility functions with well-defined contracts.

Mutation testing quantifies how well your tests detect bugs by systematically introducing small code changes (mutations) and checking whether any test fails. A mutation that survives — one your tests do not catch — indicates a missing assertion.
(TypeScript) automate this. A mutation score below
is a strong signal that your tests pass for the wrong reasons — the assertions are too weak.
The diagram below shows the four layers of the unit testing architecture for an agentic AI system, and the four categories of testable units.

attrs.alt: Unit Testing Architecture for Agentic AI Systems: pipeline from Test Suite through Test Case, Mock/Stub Layer, Agent Unit, Assertions, to PASS/FAIL; below shows four testable categories: Tool Logic, Prompt Construction, State Management, Output Parsing

content.text: (shown in amber). This layer interposes between your test cases and any I/O boundary — the Anthropic client, external APIs, the file system, the database. By replacing real I/O with deterministic stubs, every test becomes fast (no network round trips), free (no API spend), and reproducible (same output every run).

arrow from FAIL back to the Test Case represents the red-green-refactor cycle. A failing test precisely identifies the broken code path; you fix the production code, not the test, and re-run to confirm green.
Tool function validation: every function that processes input or output for an LLM tool.
Prompt construction: any function that assembles a system prompt, formats few-shot examples, or injects variables into a template.
Output parsing: functions that extract structured data from free-text LLM responses.
State management: agent memory classes, context-window truncation logic, checkpoint serialisation/deserialisation.
Error handling and retries: the branches that fire when a tool raises, when the API returns a rate-limit error, or when the model refuses a request.
Configuration and routing: any logic that selects which model, which tool set, or which prompt template to use based on runtime conditions.
Do not rely on unit tests alone for
End-to-end task correctness: whether the agent actually solves the user's problem is an eval question, not a unit test question.
Multi-component wiring: whether the HTTP route correctly hands off to the agent runner is an integration test.
Model behaviour: whether the model follows the system prompt, avoids hallucinations, or selects the right tool in ambiguous situations.
Performance under load: latency and throughput characteristics of the live system cannot be unit tested.
Structure your agent code so that the LLM client is a constructor parameter, not a module-level singleton. This is the single most impactful change for testability.
— API calls, file writes. Pure functions are trivially unit testable with no mocking required.

# Python
pip install pytest pytest-asyncio pytest-cov mutmut

# TypeScript / Node
npm install --save-dev jest ts-jest @types/jest
Step 3: Write tests for tool functions

Start with the simplest testable units: the tool functions that your agent dispatches. These are pure input-output functions — no LLM involved. Write tests for happy path, validation errors, and upstream-API errors.
Step 4: Mock the LLM client to test agent logic
With the tool functions covered, move to the agent orchestration loop. Inject a
return value to simulate text responses, tool use, and multi-turn sequences.

# agent/researcher.py
import anthropic
import json
from typing import Callable, Any

class ResearchAgent:
    """Minimal agentic loop that routes tool calls to registered handlers."""

    def __init__(
        self,
        client: anthropic.Anthropic,
        system: str = "You are a research assistant.",
        max_turns: int = 10,
    ):
        self._client = client
        self._system = system
        self._max_turns = max_turns
        self._tools: dict[str, tuple[dict, Callable]] = {}

    def register_tool(self, schema: dict, fn: Callable) -> None:
        self._tools[schema["name"]] = (schema, fn)

    def run(self, user_message: str) -> str:
        messages = [{"role": "user", "content": user_message}]
        tool_schemas = [s for s, _ in self._tools.values()]

        for _ in range(self._max_turns):
            response = self._client.messages.create(
                model="claude-sonnet-4-5",
                max_tokens=4096,
                system=self._system,
                tools=tool_schemas,
                messages=messages,
            )
            if response.stop_reason == "end_turn":
                return next(b.text for b in response.content if b.type == "text")

            if response.stop_reason == "tool_use":
                messages.append({"role": "assistant", "content": response.content})
                tool_results = []
                for block in response.content:
                    if block.type == "tool_use":
                        _, fn = self._tools[block.name]
                        result = fn(**block.input)
                        tool_results.append({
                            "type": "tool_result",
                            "tool_use_id": block.id,
                            "content": json.dumps(result) if not isinstance(result, str) else result,
                        })
                messages.append({"role": "user", "content": tool_results})

        raise RuntimeError(f"Agent exceeded max_turns={self._max_turns}")


# tests/unit/test_researcher.py
import pytest
from unittest.mock import MagicMock, call
import anthropic
from agent.researcher import ResearchAgent


def fake_text_msg(text: str) -> MagicMock:
    """Build a mock Message with stop_reason='end_turn'."""
    msg = MagicMock(spec=anthropic.types.Message)
    msg.stop_reason = "end_turn"
    block = MagicMock()
    block.type = "text"
    block.text = text
    msg.content = [block]
    return msg


def fake_tool_msg(name: str, tool_input: dict, use_id: str = "tu_01") -> MagicMock:
    """Build a mock Message with stop_reason='tool_use'."""
    msg = MagicMock(spec=anthropic.types.Message)
    msg.stop_reason = "tool_use"
    block = MagicMock()
    block.type = "tool_use"
    block.name = name
    block.input = tool_input
    block.id = use_id
    msg.content = [block]
    return msg


SEARCH_SCHEMA = {
    "name": "search_web",
    "description": "Search the web for information.",
    "input_schema": {
        "type": "object",
        "properties": {"query": {"type": "string"}},
        "required": ["query"],
    },
}


@pytest.fixture
def client():
    return MagicMock(spec=anthropic.Anthropic)


@pytest.fixture
def agent(client):
    a = ResearchAgent(client=client, max_turns=3)
    return a


class TestResearchAgentSingleTurn:
    def test_returns_text_on_end_turn(self, agent, client):
        client.messages.create.return_value = fake_text_msg("Paris is the capital of France.")
        result = agent.run("What is the capital of France?")
        assert result == "Paris is the capital of France."

    def test_calls_create_exactly_once_for_simple_question(self, agent, client):
        client.messages.create.return_value = fake_text_msg("42")
        agent.run("What is 6 times 7?")
        assert client.messages.create.call_count == 1

    def test_system_prompt_is_passed_through(self, client):
        a = ResearchAgent(client=client, system="You are a poet.")
        client.messages.create.return_value = fake_text_msg("Roses are red.")
        a.run("Write a poem.")
        _, kwargs = client.messages.create.call_args
        assert kwargs["system"] == "You are a poet."


class TestResearchAgentToolUse:
    def test_invokes_tool_function_with_correct_arguments(self, agent, client):
        client.messages.create.side_effect = [
            fake_tool_msg("search_web", {"query": "Eiffel Tower height"}, "tu_abc"),
            fake_text_msg("The Eiffel Tower is 330 m tall."),
        ]
        mock_search = MagicMock(return_value=[{"snippet": "330 metres"}])
        agent.register_tool(SEARCH_SCHEMA, mock_search)

        result = agent.run("How tall is the Eiffel Tower?")

        mock_search.assert_called_once_with(query="Eiffel Tower height")
        assert "330" in result

    def test_raises_after_max_turns(self, agent, client):
        # Always returns a tool call — agent would loop forever without the guard
        client.messages.create.return_value = fake_tool_msg(
            "search_web", {"query": "loop"}, "tu_loop"
        )
        agent.register_tool(SEARCH_SCHEMA, MagicMock(return_value=[]))

        with pytest.raises(RuntimeError, match="max_turns=3"):
            agent.run("Search infinitely")

        assert client.messages.create.call_count == 3  # stopped at max_turns
Step 5: Test prompt and output logic
Prompt construction and output parsing are pure functions that deserve thorough parametric testing. Use
to cover many input variants without duplicating test structure.

# Run tests with line + branch coverage
pytest --cov=agent --cov=tools --cov-branch --cov-report=term-missing

# Require a minimum threshold (fail CI if coverage drops)
pytest --cov=agent --cov-fail-under=85

# Run mutation testing (takes longer — run on CI nightly)
mutmut run --paths-to-mutate=agent/,tools/
mutmut results
Example 1: testing a simple calculator tool
A calculator tool has no external dependencies — it is a pure function. No mocking is needed. This is the easiest class of unit test.

This example tests a realistic multi-turn sequence: the agent calls a tool, receives the result, and then produces a final text answer. The test verifies that the message history is built correctly and that the tool result is injected in the right format.

# tests/unit/test_multi_turn.py
import pytest
from unittest.mock import MagicMock, call
import anthropic
from agent.researcher import ResearchAgent

SEARCH_SCHEMA = {
    "name": "search_web",
    "description": "Search the web.",
    "input_schema": {
        "type": "object",
        "properties": {"query": {"type": "string"}},
        "required": ["query"],
    },
}

def make_tool_use(name, input_dict, use_id):
    msg = MagicMock(spec=anthropic.types.Message)
    msg.stop_reason = "tool_use"
    block = MagicMock(type="tool_use", name=name, input=input_dict, id=use_id)
    msg.content = [block]
    return msg

def make_text(text):
    msg = MagicMock(spec=anthropic.types.Message)
    msg.stop_reason = "end_turn"
    block = MagicMock(type="text", text=text)
    msg.content = [block]
    return msg

def test_message_history_includes_tool_result_in_user_turn():
    """After a tool call the tool result must appear in a user-role message."""
    client = MagicMock(spec=anthropic.Anthropic)
    client.messages.create.side_effect = [
        make_tool_use("search_web", {"query": "quantum computing"}, "tu_001"),
        make_text("Quantum computing uses qubits."),
    ]

    agent = ResearchAgent(client=client, max_turns=5)
    search_fn = MagicMock(return_value=[{"snippet": "Qubits enable superposition."}])
    agent.register_tool(SEARCH_SCHEMA, search_fn)

    result = agent.run("Explain quantum computing.")

    # Second call to messages.create should have the tool result in messages
    second_call_messages = client.messages.create.call_args_list[1][1]["messages"]
    roles = [m["role"] for m in second_call_messages]
    assert roles == ["user", "assistant", "user"]  # initial, tool_use, tool_result

    # The third message (user turn) must contain the tool result
    tool_result_turn = second_call_messages[2]["content"]
    assert isinstance(tool_result_turn, list)
    assert tool_result_turn[0]["type"] == "tool_result"
    assert tool_result_turn[0]["tool_use_id"] == "tu_001"

    assert "Quantum" in result

Consider a production code-review agent that reads a GitHub pull request, calls a static analysis tool, and returns a structured list of findings. The agent is deployed as a GitHub Actions workflow and must process hundreds of PRs per day within a 30-second timeout.
(turns a git diff string into structured hunks), the
(converts raw model tool calls into a sorted findings list), and the

orchestration loop (manages turns, enforces the 30-second budget, handles partial failures). The team maintains 94% branch coverage on these components, with a CI gate that fails the build if coverage drops below 85%.

# agent/diff_parser.py
import re
from dataclasses import dataclass

@dataclass
class DiffHunk:
    filename: str
    start_line: int
    lines: list[str]

_FILE_HEADER = re.compile(r"""^diff --git a/(.+?) b/\1""", re.MULTILINE)
_HUNK_HEADER = re.compile(r"@@ -\d+(?:,\d+)? \+(\d+)(?:,\d+)? @@")

def parse_diff(diff_text: str) -> list[DiffHunk]:
    """Split a unified diff into per-file hunks with absolute line numbers."""
    hunks: list[DiffHunk] = []
    current_file: str | None = None
    current_start: int = 0
    current_lines: list[str] = []

    for line in diff_text.splitlines():
        file_match = _FILE_HEADER.match(line)
        if file_match:
            if current_file and current_lines:
                hunks.append(DiffHunk(current_file, current_start, current_lines))
            current_file = file_match.group(1)
            current_lines = []
            continue

        hunk_match = _HUNK_HEADER.search(line)
        if hunk_match:
            if current_lines:
                hunks.append(DiffHunk(current_file, current_start, current_lines))
            current_start = int(hunk_match.group(1))
            current_lines = []
            continue

        if line.startswith(("+", "-", " ")):
            current_lines.append(line)

    if current_file and current_lines:
        hunks.append(DiffHunk(current_file, current_start, current_lines))
    return hunks


# tests/unit/test_diff_parser.py
import pytest
from agent.diff_parser import parse_diff, DiffHunk

SIMPLE_DIFF = """\
diff --git a/src/main.py b/src/main.py
index 1234567..abcdefg 100644
--- a/src/main.py
+++ b/src/main.py
@@ -10,4 +10,5 @@
 def main():
+    print("hello")
     pass
"""

def test_parse_diff_returns_one_hunk_for_one_file():
    hunks = parse_diff(SIMPLE_DIFF)
    assert len(hunks) == 1

def test_parse_diff_extracts_correct_filename():
    hunks = parse_diff(SIMPLE_DIFF)
    assert hunks[0].filename == "src/main.py"

def test_parse_diff_extracts_start_line():
    hunks = parse_diff(SIMPLE_DIFF)
    assert hunks[0].start_line == 10

def test_parse_diff_includes_added_line():
    hunks = parse_diff(SIMPLE_DIFF)
    assert any('+    print("hello")' in line for line in hunks[0].lines)

def test_parse_diff_empty_string_returns_no_hunks():
    assert parse_diff("") == []

def test_parse_diff_handles_multiple_files():
    two_file_diff = SIMPLE_DIFF + """\ndiff --git a/tests/test_main.py b/tests/test_main.py
index aaaaaaa..bbbbbbb 100644
--- a/tests/test_main.py
+++ b/tests/test_main.py
@@ -1,2 +1,3 @@
+import pytest
 def test_placeholder(): pass
"""
    hunks = parse_diff(two_file_diff)
    filenames = {h.filename for h in hunks}
    assert "src/main.py" in filenames
    assert "tests/test_main.py" in filenames
Mistake 1: Testing the LLM instead of your code

A test that sends a real prompt to the Anthropic API and asserts on the text of the response is not a unit test — it is a manual eval disguised as a test. It is slow, expensive, and non-deterministic.

# WRONG: tests the model, not your code
def test_agent_gives_correct_capital():
    client = anthropic.Anthropic()  # real API call!
    agent = ResearchAgent(client=client)
    result = agent.run("What is the capital of France?")
    assert "Paris" in result  # might fail if model responds differently

# RIGHT: test the orchestration logic; mock the model
def test_agent_returns_model_text_on_end_turn():
    client = MagicMock(spec=anthropic.Anthropic)
    client.messages.create.return_value = fake_text_msg("Paris is the capital.")
    agent = ResearchAgent(client=client)
    result = agent.run("Capital of France?")
    assert result == "Paris is the capital."
Mistake 2: Asserting on mock internals instead of observable behaviour
Over-specifying the internal call structure (exact argument order, internal call count for unrelated calls) couples tests to implementation, making them brittle.

# WRONG: brittle — breaks if you add logging or refactor internals
def test_tool_called_with_exact_kwargs(agent, client):
    ...
    mock_tool.assert_called_once_with(
        query="python",
        max_results=5,   # hardcoded default — breaks if you change the default
        timeout=10.0,    # internal detail — the caller should not know this
    )

# RIGHT: assert on the observable contract
def test_tool_receives_query_from_model_output(agent, client):
    ...
    call_args = mock_tool.call_args
    assert call_args.kwargs["query"] == "python"  # only contract you care about
Mistake 3: One huge test per function
Writing one test that exercises many behaviours makes it impossible to tell which behaviour failed. Split each concern into its own test.

# WRONG: a single test that validates too many things at once
def test_search_web():
    with pytest.raises(ValueError): search_web("")       # empty
    with pytest.raises(ValueError): search_web("q", 0)  # bad n
    assert search_web("python") == [...]                 # happy path
    # If one assertion fails you don't know which scenario broke

# RIGHT: one scenario per test function
def test_search_web_raises_on_empty_query():
    with pytest.raises(ValueError, match="empty"):
        search_web("")

def test_search_web_raises_on_invalid_max_results():
    with pytest.raises(ValueError, match="max_results"):
        search_web("q", max_results=0)

def test_search_web_happy_path(mock_http):
    mock_http.return_value = ...
    result = search_web("python")
    assert isinstance(result, list)
Mistake 4: Missing negative and edge-case tests

It is tempting to test only the happy path. In agent code, the error branches — what happens when a tool raises, when the API returns an empty list, when the model returns a stop reason you did not expect — are often where real bugs live.

# Make sure to test these scenarios:
def test_agent_handles_empty_tool_result(agent, client):
    client.messages.create.side_effect = [
        fake_tool_msg("search_web", {"query": "obscure topic"}),
        fake_text_msg("I could not find relevant results."),
    ]
    mock_search = MagicMock(return_value=[])  # empty list
    agent.register_tool(SEARCH_SCHEMA, mock_search)
    result = agent.run("Tell me about obscure topic.")
    assert isinstance(result, str)  # agent should not crash on empty tool result

def test_agent_handles_tool_exception(agent, client):
    client.messages.create.return_value = fake_tool_msg(
        "search_web", {"query": "fail"}, "tu_err"
    )
    mock_search = MagicMock(side_effect=ConnectionError("Network unreachable"))
    agent.register_tool(SEARCH_SCHEMA, mock_search)
    with pytest.raises(ConnectionError):  # agent propagates the error
        agent.run("Search for something.")
. The name is the first line of the failure report.
It is acceptable to have multiple
statements if they all verify the same logical outcome (e.g., checking both
of an HTTP response). Do not test two independent behaviours in one test function.
Keep fixtures close to the tests that use them.
Resist the urge to centralise every fixture in
— proximity reduces the cognitive overhead of reading a test.
if you call a method that does not exist on the real class. Without
, typos in method names silently pass.
Test the error message, not just the exception type.
alone — it catches cases where the right exception is raised for the wrong reason.
. Unit tests that are properly isolated can parallelise safely and cut CI time significantly.
Keep the test suite fast enough to run on every save.
If a suite takes more than 10 seconds, developers stop running it locally. Unit tests should complete the full suite in under 5 seconds for a typical agent project.
Do not mock what you own.
Mock the Anthropic SDK boundary (the HTTP client), not your own internal classes. If
needs a test, instantiate the real
A well-structured unit test suite scales to thousands of tests without degrading developer experience. The keys are isolation, parallelism, and avoiding accidental I/O.
If a unit test takes more than 100 ms to run it is almost always performing real I/O. Common culprits in agent projects:
without a mock, which reads from environment variables and may make a network request to validate the API key.
Writing to a real temp directory inside a test, which triggers file system calls.
) at module load time, which runs on import even if the test never calls that code.
to identify the ten slowest tests in your suite. Fix them before the suite grows larger — slow tests compound.

Run unit tests on every push and pull request with a coverage gate. A typical CI pipeline progression: unit tests (< 30 s) → lint → type check → integration tests (2–5 min) → deploy-to-staging. Unit tests must pass before the heavier stages run.
— the de facto standard test runner. Offers parametrize, fixtures, plugins, and excellent error output.
— built into the standard library. Provides
with pytest for line and branch coverage reports.
, useful when you want to intercept HTTP calls at the transport layer rather than patching Python objects.
— mutation testing for Python, identifies weak assertions.
— the dominant test runner, with built-in mocking via
— TypeScript preprocessor for Jest; allows testing TypeScript source directly without a separate compile step.
— a fast alternative to Jest for projects using Vite or ESM, with a compatible API.
— intercepts fetch/axios requests at the network level, useful for mocking the Anthropic REST API without patching the SDK.
Example: async agent test with pytest-asyncio

# pip install pytest-asyncio
import pytest
import pytest_asyncio
from unittest.mock import AsyncMock, MagicMock
import anthropic
from agent.async_researcher import AsyncResearchAgent

@pytest.fixture
def async_client():
    client = MagicMock(spec=anthropic.AsyncAnthropic)
    client.messages = MagicMock()
    return client

@pytest.mark.asyncio
async def test_async_agent_returns_text(async_client):
    # AsyncMock is required for awaitable return values
    mock_response = MagicMock(spec=anthropic.types.Message)
    mock_response.stop_reason = "end_turn"
    text_block = MagicMock(type="text", text="Async response text.")
    mock_response.content = [text_block]

    async_client.messages.create = AsyncMock(return_value=mock_response)

    agent = AsyncResearchAgent(client=async_client)
    result = await agent.run("Tell me something.")
    assert result == "Async response text."
    async_client.messages.create.assert_awaited_once()
Unit testing is one layer in a testing pyramid. Understanding what each layer provides — and costs — helps you allocate testing effort correctly.
API routes, DB queries, message flow

principle recommends many unit tests (cheap, fast, numerous), fewer integration tests, and even fewer end-to-end tests. For agentic AI add a fourth layer — evals — at the top of the pyramid, covering what unit tests structurally cannot: model quality.
Prompt injection vectors in input-sanitisation functions.
If your agent prepends user input directly into a system prompt, a unit test can verify that the sanitiser strips or escapes
patterns before they reach the model.
does not appear in the assembled prompt string catches accidental credential inclusion.
is cheap to write and catches a real vulnerability.
A calculator tool that does not validate its output range could return a number that crashes downstream JSON serialisation.
Every agentic loop must have a bounded turn count. A unit test that configures
and provides an infinite-loop mock verifies that the guard actually fires. Without this test the guard is often silently omitted or misconfigured.
If your agent retries on rate-limit errors (HTTP 429), write a test that configures the mock to raise
twice and succeed on the third attempt, then assert the retry count.
An agent that calls three tools in parallel should handle the case where one tool raises while two succeed. A unit test with one mock that raises and two that return data verifies this path.
What unit tests cannot catch in security

Unit tests cannot verify that the model will not follow a prompt injection at runtime — that requires red-teaming and evals. They also cannot catch configuration errors (a wrong model ID, a missing environment variable) or supply-chain vulnerabilities in SDK dependencies. These require separate controls.
Unit tests target deterministic code only.
Mock the LLM API client so that every test is fast, free, and repeatable regardless of model behaviour.
Dependency injection is the key enabler.
Accept the Anthropic client as a constructor parameter so tests can substitute a
Four categories of agent units deserve thorough coverage:
tool functions, prompt builders, state management, and output parsers.
Branch coverage is a stronger signal than line coverage for code with many conditional branches.
Always test the max-turns guard, the error paths, and the empty-result branches.
Production failures in agentic systems most often occur in the boundary conditions, not the happy path.
to catch SDK method-name typos at test time rather than at runtime.
Unit tests are necessary but not sufficient.
They verify your orchestration logic; integration tests verify component wiring; evals verify model quality. All three layers are required for a production-grade agentic system.
Security-relevant paths deserve unit tests too:
input sanitisation, file-path validation, secret exclusion from prompts, and tool output schema enforcement are all pure-logic checks that belong in the unit test suite.
